common_misspellings.feedback: Close — but it's a double 'ss', not single 's'. The double letter is in the middle: ne-CES-sary.
Close — only ONE 'c', then double 'ss'. Pattern: ne-c-essary, then double 's': ne-CES-sary.
Last syllable is '-ary', not '-ery'. Necess-ARY.

exercises.example_sentence: It is necessary to bring your passport.

common_misspellings.feedback: Close — but it's double 'ff' in the middle: di-FF-erent.
No final 'e' in English. Just 'different'.
Close — don't drop the 'e' in the middle: dif-fer-ent (three syllables).

exercises.prompt_definition_ru: интересный (что-то, что хочется узнать или посмотреть)
exercises.prompt_definition_en: engaging; making you want to know more

common_misspellings.feedback: Don't drop the second 'e' — it's IN-TER-EST-ing (four syllables, all vowels written).
Missing final 'g'. The -ing ending always keeps the 'g': interestinG.
Missing the 'e' between 'inter' and 'sting'. Inter-EST-ing.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Spelled i-n-t-e-r-e-s-t-i-n-g. Four syllables: in-ter-est-ing. Don't drop the middle 'e'.

exercises.prompt_definition_en: place where you go to eat a meal

common_misspellings.feedback: Russian 'ресторан' has 'o', but English 'restaurant' has 'AU' — silent 'u' after the 'a'.
Letters in wrong order — it's res-tau-rant (au, not au+a).
Missing the 'a' before 'nt'. Spelled res-tau-r-A-nt.
It's 'au' (rest-AU-rant), not 'ou'.

exercises.example_sentence: We had dinner at an Italian restaurant.
exercises.fallback_feedback: Spelled r-e-s-t-a-u-r-a-n-t. The tricky part is the silent 'u' after 'a': rest-AU-rant.

exercises.prompt_definition_en: the one you like the best

common_misspellings.feedback: Russian 'фаворит' is shorter. English needs 'favOURite' (British) or 'favorite' (American). Both end in '-ite'.
Ends in '-ite', not '-ate'. Fav-our-ITE.
Letters in wrong order — it's 'favourite' (fav-OUR-ite), not 'favoirite'.
Accepted (American spelling). British/standard for our family is 'favourite' with 'u'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Spelled f-a-v-o-u-r-i-t-e (British) or f-a-v-o-r-i-t-e (American). Either works — but always ends in '-ite', never '-it' or '-ate'.

exercises.prompt_definition_en: physically nice to wear, sit on, or be in

common_misspellings.feedback: Starts with 'c', not 'k'. Russian 'комфортный' uses 'к', but English uses 'C': COM-fort-able.
Don't drop the 'a'. It's com-fort-ABLE (three syllables, ending '-able').
Ends in '-able', not '-eble'. The suffix is always '-able' here.
Missing 'm' — it's COM-fort-able, not 'con-fort-able'.

exercises.fallback_feedback: Spelled c-o-m-f-o-r-t-a-b-l-e. Starts with 'c' (not 'k'), ends in '-able' (not '-eble').
